name: Planet Labs PBC

profile.description: Planet Labs PBC designs, builds, and operates the world's largest fleet of Earth-imaging satellites, delivering daily global imagery at up to 50cm resolution through SuperDove, SkySat, Pelican, and Tanager constellations. Serves agriculture, defense, energy, forestry, finance, and government sectors via an AI-powered analytics platform. A Public Benefit Corporation founded in 2010, headquartered in San Francisco, with approximately $18B market cap.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (2/3): Planet launched the Pelican next-generation high-resolution satellite constellation and the Tanager hyperspectral imaging satellite in 2024-2025, expanding capabilities from optical to full-spectrum imaging [1]. Defense & Intelligence revenue surged 70% YoY in Q3 FY2026 to $49.4M, representing a successful pivot toward higher-value government contracts [1]. AI Deployment (2/2): Planet deploys AI-powered analytics for automated change detection, crop classification, and deforestation monitoring, with CEO Will Marshall stating AI will 'change the status quo' for satellite imagery analysis [3]. The company is building 'Large Earth Models' trained on the physical history of Earth — a proprietary AI data advantage [3]. Market Share (2/2): Planet operates the largest commercial Earth-imaging fleet globally with 200+ satellites providing daily global coverage, a capability no competitor matches at scale; FY2026 revenue reached a record $307.7M, up 26% YoY [1]. R&D Intensity (1/1): Planet invests heavily in satellite design, AI analytics, and platform development relative to its revenue base, consistent with a capital-intensive aerospace innovator [2].

inspiration.label: DEVELOPING
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Planet's mission — 'to accelerate humanity's understanding of Earth to drive action' — is specific, purposeful, and deeply connected to its business model of daily global imaging [2]. As a Public Benefit Corporation, the company has a statutory duty to balance profit with stakeholder impact, and the mission genuinely shapes product development (environmental monitoring, humanitarian applications). However, the company is 16 years old and has not yet proven multi-decade mission consistency through leadership transitions. Community Impact (1/2): Planet partners with Global Heritage Fund to protect cultural heritage in Ukraine and provides data for disaster response and environmental monitoring applications [4]. The PBC structure and commitment to seeking 'the greatest good' signal genuine community orientation. Employee Retention (1/2): Founding team (Will Marshall, Robbie Schingler) remains intact since 2010, signaling leadership stability across 945 specialized employees in aerospace and data science [2]. No specific turnover data publicly available. Insider Buying (1/2): CEO Will Marshall and co-founder Robbie Schingler maintain significant equity positions as founders, though no notable net insider buying beyond standard compensation was identified in the last 12 months.

infrastructure.label: DEVELOPING
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. Planet achieved its first full fiscal year of non-GAAP profitability in FY2026 with adjusted EBITDA of $15.5M and free cash flow of $52.9M, but remains deeply GAAP unprofitable with profit margin of -80.2% and negative ROE of -78.4% [1]. Revenue growth is strong at 26% YoY and 17.2% 3-year CAGR, earning full marks on both growth metrics. D/E of 245.4% reflects significant convertible debt leverage, scoring 0.24. Forward P/E is negative (GAAP unprofitable), scoring 0. No PEG available. FY2027 guidance of $415-440M (midpoint ~39% growth) with $900M backlog (79% YoY growth) provides strong revenue visibility [1]. Calculation: (0.24x1 + 0x2 + 1.0x1 + 1.0x2 + 0x1 + 0x1) / 8 x 10 = 4.

ai_resilience.label: LOW RISK
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (7/9): Planet's core business — operating 200+ physical satellites in orbit, processing petabytes of daily imagery, and maintaining a proprietary multi-year global dataset — is structurally immune to AI disruption [2]. No AI model can replace physical satellite infrastructure, launch operations, or the irreplaceable time-series archive of daily Earth imagery. AI actually increases demand for Planet's data as the training substrate for geospatial models. The Defense & Intelligence segment depends on classified government contracts and security clearances that AI cannot substitute [1]. Scores PROTECTED-to-IMMUNE because while the physical infrastructure is safe, competition from other satellite operators using AI analytics could pressure pricing. AI Opportunity (1/1): Planet actively generates AI-driven revenue through its analytics platform, automated change detection, and 'Large Earth Models' initiative [3]. CEO Will Marshall describes Planet's dataset as the ideal substrate for AI consumption.

infinite_game.summary: Planet Labs exhibits strong infinite game characteristics rooted in its PBC structure and founder-led mission. The company has maintained a consistent purpose — imaging Earth daily to drive understanding and action — since founding in 2010, through SPAC listing, multiple satellite generation launches, and years of sustained losses. The willingness to sustain years of unprofitability while building a unique global infrastructure asset reflects genuine long-term thinking, though the company has not yet been tested through a true leadership transition or existential crisis.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Planet's cause — accelerating humanity's understanding of Earth — is genuine, distinctive, and shapes every strategic decision from satellite design to data platform development [2]. The PBC structure legally embeds this purpose into corporate governance. However, the company was founded in 2010 and has not yet demonstrated multi-decade consistency or survived a leadership transition — the cause is real but partially validated.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Planet's founding team remains intact — Will Marshall (CEO) and Robbie Schingler (CSO) have led since 2010 [2]. The 945-person team operates in a mission-driven culture of rocket scientists and data engineers who 'get excited by hard problems.' No evidence of layoffs, cultural instability, or internal conflict. Company is small enough that cultural assessment data is limited, but leadership continuity is a positive signal.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): Planet competes with Maxar, Airbus Defence, and emerging NewSpace startups but frames its differentiation around daily global coverage frequency rather than defeating competitors [2]. The approach of complementing government satellite programs (Landsat, Sentinel) rather than competing demonstrates an infinite competitive mindset focused on expanding the total addressable market for geospatial intelligence.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (1/2): Planet has evolved from a small-satellite imaging company into a full-stack data platform with AI analytics, hyperspectral imaging (Tanager), and satellite-as-a-service offerings [2]. The pivot from pure imagery to analytics and government services represents meaningful business model evolution. However, this is incremental rather than the transformative reinvention Sinek describes — no equivalent of abandoning a core profitable business.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (1/2): Planet sustained years of operating losses ($80M+ annually) to build the world's largest commercial satellite fleet — a bet that required deep conviction the market would eventually value daily global imaging [1]. Continuing heavy investment in Pelican and Tanager during a punishing environment for growth stocks (2022-2023) demonstrated willingness to prioritize long-term capability over near-term financial metrics.

investment_thesis: Planet Labs delivered a breakout FY2026 with $307.7M revenue (up 26% YoY), its first full year of non-GAAP profitability ($15.5M adjusted EBITDA), and positive free cash flow of $52.9M [1]. Q4 FY2026 revenue hit $86.8M (up 41% YoY), accelerating through the year. Defense & Intelligence surged 70% YoY in Q3, and end-of-period backlog reached ~$900M (up 79% YoY), providing strong visibility into FY2027 guidance of $415-440M (~39% growth) [1]. The company operates an irreplaceable physical asset — 200+ satellites delivering daily global imagery — that becomes more valuable as AI drives exponential demand for geospatial training data. The 'Large Earth Models' initiative positions Planet at the intersection of space data and AI [3].
thesis_continued: The bear case centers on valuation and profitability path. At ~$18B market cap on $308M revenue (~59x sales), Planet is priced for sustained hypergrowth despite remaining deeply GAAP unprofitable with -80.2% profit margins and D/E of 245.4% from convertible debt [5]. Non-GAAP profitability adjustments exclude substantial stock-based compensation that represents real dilution. Concentration in Defense & Intelligence (the fastest-growing segment) creates vulnerability to government budget cycles and contract timing. Competition from Maxar (Advent-owned), Airbus Defence & Space, and emerging NewSpace competitors (BlackSky, Satellogic) could pressure pricing in commercial markets. The $900M backlog is encouraging but untested against potential cancellation clauses or budget sequestration.
key_catalysts: FY2027 revenue guidance of $415-440M with ~$900M backlog providing 2+ years of visibility, driven by accelerating Defense & Intelligence adoption and AI-powered analytics revenue growth
Pelican high-resolution constellation deployment expanding addressable market into sub-meter commercial and government applications previously served primarily by Maxar
Large Earth Models initiative creating a proprietary AI platform trained on the world's largest daily geospatial dataset, potentially commanding premium pricing for AI-derived analytics services
key_risks: Valuation of ~59x revenue requires sustained 30%+ growth and a credible path to GAAP profitability — any growth deceleration or margin miss could trigger severe multiple compression given -80% GAAP margins
Heavy dependence on Defense & Intelligence segment creates concentration risk tied to U.S. government budget cycles, continuing resolution uncertainty, and geopolitical priority shifts
Convertible debt driving 245% D/E ratio creates dilution risk for equity holders, and stock-based compensation materially inflates non-GAAP profitability metrics versus GAAP reality
